concerns.text: The central claim of 'Attribution Hallucination' relies on SAA scores derived from an automated LLM judge (Qwen3-VL-235B). While Appendix Table 4 shows a Friedman test p-value > 0.05 against human experts, the sample size for this validation is only N=200 (Section 'Details of Expert Evaluation'). This is statistically underpowered to validate a complex, multi-dimensional metric (SAA) across 20 different models. The paper must report the confidence intervals for the inter-rater reliability (e.g., Cohen's Kappa) and the specific breakdown of disagreements to prove the automated judge is robust enough to support the main claims.

The 'Crucial Evidence' ground truth is generated via an automated ablation pipeline (Section 3.3) using a single model (Qwen3-VL-235B). There is no reported inter-annotator agreement or human verification of the 'Crucial' labels themselves, only the final QA pairs. If the ground truth definition of 'crucial' is noisy or model-biased, the SAA metric (which depends on it) is invalid. The paper must provide evidence of human validation on the 'Crucial Evidence' labels, not just the final answers.

Table 1 compares 20 models, but the input resolution varies significantly (Native API vs. 150 DPI vs. downsampled to 1024x1024 or 768x768). The paper claims these differences are 'adapted' but does not provide a controlled experiment isolating the effect of resolution on SAA for a single model. The performance gap between closed-source (Native API) and open-source (downsampled) models may be an artifact of input fidelity rather than model capability, confounding the 'Attribution Hallucination' conclusion.

The statistical significance of the 'Attribution Hallucination' gap (e.g., Ans. 87.1 vs SAA 59.0 for GPT-5.4) is not tested. The paper reports point estimates without confidence intervals or p-values for the differences between metrics or models. Given the sample size (N=1,897), a bootstrap analysis or paired t-test is required to confirm these gaps are not due to random variance.
